Young and early-career editors

Teen Video Editor Jobs

Teen editors can build real proof through school media, clubs, local businesses, creator projects, internships, and assistant-style editing work, but expectations should be clear and age-appropriate.

This page captures teen editor search demand while routing young editors toward safer portfolio-building and clearly scoped opportunities.

Join editor alerts Post a role

Good first editing opportunities

Strong early fits include school sports clips, club recaps, podcast cleanup, local business social videos, nonprofit projects, creator shorts, campus media, and assistant tasks with a clear adult point of contact.

What teen editors should protect

Ask for written scope, pay or credit, deadline, revision expectations, where finished work can be shown, and whether local rules or guardian approval apply before taking paid client work.

Role examples

Editing work this page routes toward

Remote

Short-form video editor

Reels, TikTok, YouTube Shorts, creator teams

  • fast turnaround
  • caption styling
  • social hooks
Remote or hybrid

YouTube channel editor

Long-form YouTube, podcasts, education, creator-led brands

  • story pacing
  • retention edits
  • thumbnail collaboration
Contract

Brand video editor

B2B, ecommerce, product launches, paid social

  • motion basics
  • ad variants
  • stakeholder review
Entry level

Assistant editor

Post houses, agencies, production teams

  • media management
  • proxy workflows
  • Premiere or Resolve

Answers

Questions editors and hiring teams ask first

Can teenagers get video editor jobs?

Yes, especially project-based and portfolio-building work, but young editors should keep expectations written down, protect school schedules, and follow local rules for paid work.

What should a teen video editor portfolio include?

Short clips, before-and-after examples, school or club work, spec edits, captions, audio cleanup, and a short note explaining the editor's exact role on each piece.
